claims.claim_text: The opening paragraph presents a January 2024 fintech outage with specific timing, architecture mismatch details, and a 22-minute recovery window.
claims.anchor_text: In January 2024, a mid-stage fintech startup pushed a routine update to their payment processing service. The deployment succeeded. Five minutes later, their monitoring exploded. The application was crashing on startup, throwing cryptic "exec format error" messages. The previous container image -- the one that worked -- had been overwritten because they were using the `latest` tag with mutable tagging enabled. Their CI pipeline had pushed an ARM64 image over the existing AMD64 image. No versioning. No immutability. No way to roll back except to rebuild from source, which took 22 minutes while their payment pipeline was down. Twenty-two minutes of lost transactions for a fintech company is the kind of thing that ends up in board meeting slides.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A bad container-image tagging workflow can turn a routine deployment into an incident: if a new image overwrites the tag your platform expects, rollback becomes slower, recovery gets harder, and rebuilding a known-good image may take longer than your team can afford.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed war story with fake-precise dates, timings, and operational details.

claims.claim_text: Each AWS account gets a default private ECR registry per Region, and the private registry URI format is `<account>.dkr.ecr.<region>.amazonaws.com`.
claims.rationale: AWS private registry documentation directly describes the default-registry model and URI format.

claims.claim_text: The module states absolutely that the private registry URL cannot be changed.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Use the registry URI AWS assigns for your account and Region.
claims.rationale: The documentation clearly defines the default URI format, but I did not find a primary AWS page that phrases this exact absolute as written.

claims.claim_text: The ECR Public pull URI format is `public.ecr.aws/{alias}`.
claims.rationale: AWS public-registry documentation explicitly gives the public pull URI format.

claims.claim_text: ECR Public pushes require authentication, while pulls from public repositories can be anonymous.
claims.rationale: AWS ECR Public documentation directly states that pushes require authentication and pulls may be anonymous.

claims.claim_text: Private ECR storage is billed at $0.10 per GB-month and public repositories are free up to AWS's published limits.
claims.rationale: The AWS pricing page directly supports the quoted private-storage rate and the public free-tier limits framing.

claims.claim_text: The table says ECR Public is `us-east-1` only.
claims.anchor_text: | Regions | All commercial regions | us-east-1 only (content delivered globally via CloudFront) |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Regions | Regional service | Check the current AWS ECR Public endpoints page for public-side Region details |
claims.rationale: AWS currently publishes ECR Public endpoints in more than one Region, so the `us-east-1 only` claim is outdated.

claims.claim_text: The table states categorically that ECR Public does not support vulnerability scanning.
claims.anchor_text: | Vulnerability scanning | Basic + Enhanced (Inspector) | Not supported |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Vulnerability scanning | Basic + Enhanced (Inspector) | Check current ECR Public docs before assuming private-registry scanning features apply |
claims.rationale: I did not find a current AWS primary page that directly supports this exact public-side `Not supported` cell as written.

claims.claim_text: The table states categorically that ECR Public does not support lifecycle policies.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Lifecycle policies | Yes | Check current ECR Public docs before assuming private-registry lifecycle-policy features apply |
claims.rationale: The private-side lifecycle-policy feature is well documented, but I did not find a direct AWS primary source for this exact public-side `No` cell.

claims.claim_text: The ECR authorization token used by `get-login-password` is valid for 12 hours.
claims.rationale: AWS authentication documentation explicitly states the token validity period.

claims.claim_text: The module says ECS always uses the task execution role for pulls and that EKS nodes use the instance profile or IRSA to authenticate to ECR.
claims.anchor_text: For ECS and EKS workloads, you do not need to handle authentication manually. ECS automatically pulls images from ECR using the task execution role. EKS nodes use the instance profile or IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts) to authenticate.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For ECS and EKS workloads, you often do not handle Docker authentication manually, but the exact IAM role depends on the launch type and platform you are using.
claims.rationale: AWS documents different ECR pull roles for ECS on EC2, Fargate, and EKS nodes, so the original sentence overgeneralizes and partly misstates the EKS model.

claims.claim_text: With tag immutability enabled, an existing tag cannot be overwritten and a tag keeps pointing at the same image.
claims.rationale: AWS tag-mutability documentation directly supports the overwrite-prevention behavior.

claims.claim_text: The prose says immutability and `latest` are fundamentally incompatible in all cases.
claims.span_hint: section: Image Tagging Strategies and Immutability
claims.anchor_text: A practical note on immutability and `latest`: they are fundamentally incompatible. If you enable immutability, you cannot push `latest` more than once.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A practical note on immutability and `latest`: decide explicitly whether `latest` should move over time or whether the repository should stay fully immutable.
claims.rationale: Current ECR supports immutable repositories with tag exclusion filters, so the original absolute statement is too strong.

claims.claim_text: The module says ECR Basic scanning uses Clair and is included at no extra cost.
claims.anchor_text: Basic scanning uses the open-source Clair engine to check for known CVEs in OS packages. It is included in ECR at no extra cost.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Basic scanning checks container images for operating-system package vulnerabilities. For deeper coverage of programming-language packages, use enhanced scanning with Amazon Inspector.
claims.rationale: AWS's current basic-scanning documentation says it uses AWS-native technology rather than Clair, so the original engine claim is outdated.

claims.claim_text: Enhanced scanning integrates with Amazon Inspector, scans both OS and programming-language package vulnerabilities, and incurs Inspector charges.
claims.rationale: AWS enhanced-scanning docs directly describe the Inspector integration, broader package coverage, and billing note.

claims.claim_text: The lifecycle-policy section uses a specific build cadence and current storage price to estimate image growth and cost.
claims.anchor_text: A team pushing 10 builds per day generates 300+ images per month per repository. At $0.10/GB/month, this adds up.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Even a modest CI cadence can accumulate a large backlog of images over time, so retention rules matter for both operational hygiene and storage cost.
claims.rationale: The exact build-rate math and price point are illustrative teaching numbers rather than stable evergreen facts.

claims.claim_text: ECR lifecycle rules are evaluated across all rules and then applied by priority, and lower-priority rules cannot expire an image already claimed by a higher-priority rule.
claims.span_hint: section: Lifecycle Policy Rule Evaluation Order
claims.rationale: AWS lifecycle-policy documentation explicitly describes evaluation timing and rule-priority behavior.

claims.claim_text: The module says cross-region replication avoids startup latency and transfer costs from cross-region pulls.
claims.anchor_text: ECR supports automatic replication of images to other regions. This is essential for multi-region deployments to avoid cross-region image pulls during container startup (which add latency and data transfer costs).
claims.suggested_rewrite: ECR supports automatic replication of images to other regions. This is useful for multi-region deployments because it keeps image distribution aligned with where your workloads run.
claims.rationale: AWS directly documents replication and inter-Region transfer charges, but the original sentence adds a latency assertion not directly backed on the cited primary pages.

claims.claim_text: Cross-account replication requires a destination-side registry policy granting `ecr:ReplicateImage`, and `ecr:CreateRepository` if repositories should be created automatically.
claims.rationale: AWS replication-permissions documentation directly lists the required actions and when `CreateRepository` is needed.

claims.claim_text: For private ECR pulls over PrivateLink, AWS documents separate `ecr.api` and `ecr.dkr` endpoints plus an S3 endpoint because image layers are stored in S3.
claims.span_hint: section: Securing ECR with VPC Endpoints (AWS PrivateLink)
claims.rationale: AWS VPC endpoint documentation explicitly names the endpoint set and explains the S3 layer-download requirement.

claims.claim_text: The module says missing the S3 endpoint leaves ECS tasks hanging indefinitely in `PENDING`.
claims.span_hint: section: Securing ECR with VPC Endpoints (AWS PrivateLink)
claims.anchor_text: If you block public internet access in your private subnets and forget the S3 Gateway Endpoint, your ECS tasks will authenticate successfully but hang indefinitely in the "PENDING" state while trying to download the image layers.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If you block public internet access in your private subnets, make sure the S3 endpoint path is in place as well as the ECR endpoints.
claims.rationale: AWS documents the S3 dependency, but I did not find a primary source for this exact `PENDING` symptom wording.

claims.claim_text: The `Did You Know` bullet claims specific ECR storage internals and a 40-60% savings figure from deduplication.
claims.anchor_text: 1. **ECR stores images in S3 under the hood**, but you cannot see or access the S3 buckets directly. Each image layer is stored as an individual S3 object, deduplicated across all repositories in the same account and region. If five of your repositories use the same base layer (like `ubuntu:22.04`), that layer is stored only once. This deduplication can reduce your storage costs by 40-60% for organizations with many similar images.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 1. **Shared base layers can reduce duplicate storage and upload work**, so standardizing images across repositories has real operational benefits.
claims.rationale: AWS supports same-registry layer reuse, but the inaccessible-bucket phrasing and 40-60% savings figure are not directly backed on a current primary page.

claims.claim_text: The ECR credential helper can be configured so Docker uses AWS credentials for ECR without repeated manual `docker login` calls.
claims.rationale: The upstream credential-helper README explicitly says there is no need to use `docker login` once the helper is configured.

claims.claim_text: The module says GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins all use the same credential-helper mechanism under the hood for ECR.
claims.anchor_text: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins all have native ECR integration that uses this same mechanism under the hood.
claims.suggested_rewrite: CI systems can authenticate to ECR in different ways, so check the integration you actually use instead of assuming they all work the same way.
claims.rationale: That cross-platform implementation claim is too broad to support from a single primary source.

claims.claim_text: ECR pull-through cache supports upstream registries such as Docker Hub, Quay, and GitHub Container Registry.
claims.rationale: AWS pull-through-cache documentation directly lists the supported upstream registries.

claims.claim_text: Docker Hub anonymous users are limited to 100 pulls per 6 hours.
claims.rationale: Docker's own usage-and-limits documentation directly states the current anonymous pull limit.

claims.claim_text: The Amazon Inspector bullet claims `15+` supported languages and gives an unsupported `3` versus `28` vulnerability example.
claims.anchor_text: 4. **Amazon Inspector's enhanced scanning for ECR can detect vulnerabilities in 15+ programming languages**, not just OS packages. This includes npm, pip, Maven, NuGet, Go modules, Rust crates, and more. A single Node.js application image might have 3 OS-level vulnerabilities but 28 application-level ones. Basic scanning would only catch the 3.
claims.suggested_rewrite: 4. **Enhanced scanning covers more than basic OS-only scanning**, especially when your images include application dependencies.
claims.rationale: Amazon Inspector documents supported ecosystems, but the `15+` count and the `3` versus `28` example are not directly backed as written.

claims.claim_text: In ECR basic scanning, repositories are manual-scan by default until you configure scan-on-push filters.
claims.rationale: AWS basic-scanning configuration docs explicitly say private registries default to manual scanning unless scan-on-push filters are configured.

claims.claim_text: The common-mistakes table says cross-region pulls add `200-500ms` to startup and incur data transfer charges.
claims.anchor_text: | Pulling images cross-region in production | The image exists in us-east-1 but the ECS cluster is in eu-west-1 | Configure ECR replication to all regions where you deploy containers. Cross-region pulls add 200-500ms to container startup and incur data transfer charges |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Pulling images cross-region in production | The image exists in us-east-1 but the ECS cluster is in eu-west-1 | Replicate images to the Regions where you deploy so startup does not depend on inter-Region pulls |
claims.rationale: AWS pricing supports the transfer-cost part, but I did not find a primary source for the exact `200-500ms` latency number.

claims.claim_text: The common-mistakes table recommends a specific default lifecycle policy of 20 tagged images and 3 days for untagged images as broadly suitable.
claims.anchor_text: | No lifecycle policy | Teams do not think about storage costs until the bill arrives | Apply lifecycle policies at repository creation time. A default policy that keeps the last 20 tagged images and removes untagged images after 3 days works for most teams |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | No lifecycle policy | Teams often postpone cleanup until image sprawl becomes visible | Apply lifecycle policies early and tune retention to your deployment cadence and rollback requirements |
claims.rationale: Those retention numbers are opinionated operational defaults, not a generally supportable fact for `most teams`.

further_reading.title: Amazon ECR private registry
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers the core registry model, private registry URI format, and account-and-Region basics.

further_reading.title: Scan images for software vulnerabilities in Amazon ECR
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains the current Basic versus Enhanced scanning model and where Amazon Inspector fits.

further_reading.title: Amazon ECR interface VPC endpoints (AWS PrivateLink)
further_reading.why_relevant: Documents the endpoint combination needed to pull private images without internet egress.

notes: Primary sources were current AWS ECR and Amazon Inspector docs, plus Docker's own rate-limit docs. Several public-ECR and scanning details in the module are outdated as of 2026-04-23 UTC.
